DEFENDANT TIMEC SERVICES COMPANY, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR ISSUANCE OF TENTATIVE RULING ONLY)
Off Calendar Oct-03-2023 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES: (PART 2 OF 2)
(Mini Minutes continued from previous entry)

Defendant offers Decedent's 2018 workers' compensation deposition to show Plaintiffs' allegations also pertained to Defendant's workers or contractors, so Plaintiffs knew in June 2020 that Defendant was in fact responsible for asbestos removal. Decedent testified that: Plant Maintenance and "Tie Mech" employees were generally around at the Exxon jobsite (Defendant's Index of Exhibits, Ex. N [Decedent's Aug. 2018 Workers' Comp. Depo.] at 63:20-23, 62:20-22); "they" were laborers, and "they" generally removed asbestos (63:24-64:1); the employees removed asbestos-containing insulation and gaskets (62:23-63:2); the employees stirred up and swept up any dust they created (63:3-7); and he believed the dust contained asbestos (63:6-10).

The 2018 workers' compensation deposition is insufficient to sustain Defendant's initial burden. There were no further references to Defendant after Decedent mentioned the "Tie Mech" workers. (See Defendant's Index of Exhibits, Ex. N [Decedent's Aug. 2018 Workers' Comp. Depo.] at 64:5-16.) Decedent was later asked only about the Plant Maintenance workers he saw and how far away they were from Decedent when they removed asbestos insulation (Ex. N at 64:5-7), as well as approximately how many instances Decedent had observed Plant Maintenance workers removing insulation less than 20 feet from him (Ex. N at 64:10-12).

In June 2020, Plaintiffs only knew that Defendant may have hired the workers or contractors that removed the asbestos-containing insulation or gaskets at Exxon. Section 474 did not impose a duty on Plaintiffs to exercise reasonable diligence to obtain facts they should have known. (Hahn, supra, 77 Cal.App.5th at p. 900.) Plaintiffs waited to name Defendant until they had a sufficient basis to allege Defendant's activities exposed Decedent to asbestos. (See Defendant's Index of Exhibits, Ex. D [Counsel Decl. for Ex Parte Appl. Re: Doe Amendment] at 1:27-28, 2:1-5.) As such, Defendant fails to show Plaintiffs knew sufficient facts in June 2020 that Defendant exposed Decedent to asbestos such that the statute of limitations barred the wrongful death case against Defendant at the time of the Doe amendment in August 2021. Order signed in open court. Judge: Samuel K. Feng, Clerk: A. Mok. Reporter: Teresa Kenworthy, CSR #6673, tkenworthy@gmail.com (Tel: 530-903-2088) (503/SKF)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Oct-03-2023 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES Via CourtCall:
Christopher B. Bruni of BERKES CRANE SANTANA & SPANGLER LLP (213-955-1150), appearing for defendant and moving party Timec Services Company, Inc., Formerly Known As Broadspectrum Downstream Services, Inc.
Jason M. Rose of BRAYTON & PURCELL (415-898-1555), appearing for plaintiff and opposing party Teresa Starr, et al.

RULING:
Hearing held, argued and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant Timec Services Company, Inc., Formerly Known As Broadspectrum Downstream Services, Inc.'s Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiff. Reply filed by Defendant.

Defendant establishes newly discovered facts to support the issues reasserted in this subsequent summary judgment motion. (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 437c, subd. (f)(2); Patterson v. Sacramento City Unified School Dist. (2007) 155 Cal.App.4th 821, 827.) Defendant "did not have and had not seen [Decedent's 2018 workers' compensation deposition] at the time of the August 18, 2022 hearing on its initial summary judgment motion." (Bruni Decl., par. 16 [4:15-17].) Plaintiffs' objection to the motion is OVERRULED.

The Court considered Exhibits A through M in Defendant's Index of Exhibits and OVERRULED Plaintiffs' objections on untimely reconsideration grounds. The Court considered Exhibit N in Defendant's Index of Exhibits and OVERRULED Plaintiffs' objection on hearsay grounds. (See Hahn v. New York Air Brake, LLC (2022) 77 Cal.App.5th 895, 899 [noting that the moving party relied on deposition testimony].) The Court considered Paragraph 16 of the Bruni Declaration and OVERRULED Plaintiffs' objection on hearsay and lack of foundation/personal knowledge grounds. The Court declines to rule on Defendant's objections to the Rose Declaration. The objections are not material to the motion's disposition. (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 437c, subd. (q).)

Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden that Plaintiffs' cause of action has no merit by showing the statute of limitations is a complete defense. (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 437c, subd. (p)(2); see Anderson v. Metalclad Insulation Corp. (1999) 72 Cal.App.4th 284, 289 [explaining initial burden on complete defense ground].) Defendant must show Plaintiffs were not ignorant, for purposes of section 474 of the Code of Civil Procedure, at the time of the Doe amendment because Plaintiffs knew enough facts to state a cause of action earlier, regardless of whether they subjectively understood the legal significance of the facts. (Hahn, supra, 77 Cal.App.5th at p. 900.) "If the defendant fails to . . . produce substantial evidence supporting [any] element [of the defense], the trial court cannot properly grant summary judgment." (Consumer Cause, Inc. v. SmileCare (2001) 91 Cal.App.4th 454, 468.)

Before filing the wrongful death complaint in June 2020, Plaintiffs knew certain entities were responsible for asbestos removal at Decedent's jobsites. (See Defendant's Index of Exhibits, Ex. B [June 2020 Wrongful Death Compl.] at par. 56 [21:23-22:3].) Plaintiffs allege that at certain jobsite premises, workers and/or various unqualified or unskilled contractors caused the release of dangerous quantities of toxic asbestos fibers into the ambient air and created a hazardous and unsafe condition to Decedent. (Id.) As an example, Plaintiffs allege Temporary Plant Cleaners, Inc. (and Plant Maintenance, Inc. of California as an alternative entity) was one such entity that hired the workers or contractors. (See id. at par. 53 [17:26-27, 17-9:19].)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Mar-10-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Kimberly J. Chu of BRAYTON & PURCELL LLP (415-898-1555), appearing for plaintiff via CourtCall. No other appearances on this matter.

Ruling:
Not contested and the Court adopted its tentative ruling:
Defendant Parsons Government Services Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is DENIED. Defendant failed to sustain its burden of showing that Plaintiffs do not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence that decedent Michael Starr was exposed to asbestos containing products attributable to Defendant. (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal. 4th 826, 855.) Co-worker witness Paul Schulz testified that he and decedent were present when Defendant's employees removed one to two feet of pipe insulation from a six to eight diameter pipe. (Undisputed Material Fact 16 [Hopwood Declaration, Ex. H at 317:21-318:19].) He also testified that it took 15 minutes at most to take the insulation off. (Undisputed Material Fact 17 [Id. at 321:24-322:13].) Further, that was the only time he recalled decedent being present while Defendant's employees were working, other than walking past. (Undisputed Material Fact 18 [Id. at 332:22-333:22].) Plaintiffs properly used Mr. Starr's trial preservation transcript to refresh Mr. Schulz's memory. The admissibility of Mr. Starr's transcript is irrelevant. (Evans v. Hood Corp. (2015) 5 Cal. App. 5th 1022, 1042 ["documents used to refresh a witness's recollection need not be admissible and indeed are inadmissible under Evidence Code section 771, subdivision (b), except when proffered by the adverse party"].) Order signed in open court. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee; Matter Not Reported (503/CML)
